Massol & Crochet 2008 — does Wolf's syndrome depend on β?

Source: vignettes/paper-massol-crochet-2008.Rmd
paper-massol-crochet-2008.Rmd

A direct test in clade of Massol & Crochet’s (2008) critique of Wolf et al. 2007: the dimorphism in exploration intensity x depends critically on the trade-off shape parameter β in g(x) = (1-x)^β. Implemented as a β sweep over the spatially-explicit Wolf 2007 scenario added in 0.7.0. Companion to vignette("paper-wolf2007").

The critique

Wolf et al. (2007) report a stable dimorphism in exploration intensity x under disruptive selection at year-2 reproduction. The trade-off between year-1 and year-2 fecundity is parameterised by

g(x)=(1−x)β g(x) = (1 - x)^\beta

so that year-1 fecundity scales as (1-x)^β · f_low and year-2 fecundity as x · f_high (plus the (1-x) leg). Wolf uses β = 1.25.

Massol & Crochet (2008, Nature 451, brief communications arising; doi:10.1038/nature06744) argued that this dimorphism is not robust: shift β much above or below Wolf’s value and the bimodal x distribution collapses. Their critique is mathematical (the conditions for stability of the mixed equilibrium have a narrow β range) but easy to test in a simulation.

clade’s Wolf scenario exposes β as personality_beta. Sweeping it across [0.5, 3.0] at fixed seed gives a direct probe of the critique.

The sweep 

library(clade)

run_one <- function(beta_val, seed = 42L) {
  s <- wolf_personality_specs()
  s$personality_beta                <- beta_val
  s$max_ticks                       <- 5000L
  s$random_seed                     <- seed
  s$n_agents_init                   <- 200L
  s$max_agents                      <- 800L
  s$personality_hawkdove_per_tick   <- 0.3
  s$personality_hawkdove_radius     <- 2L
  s$n_predators_init                <- 10L
  s$predator_max_agents             <- 30L
  env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)

  recs  <- env$agents
  alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive),
                  logical(1L))
  if (sum(alive) < 30L) return(NULL)

  ex <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],
               function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$exploration), numeric(1L))
  bo <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],
               function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$boldness), numeric(1L))
  ag <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],
               function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$aggressiveness), numeric(1L))
  list(
    beta   = beta_val,
    n      = length(ex),
    r_eb   = suppressWarnings(cor(ex, bo)),
    r_ea   = suppressWarnings(cor(ex, ag)),
    r_ba   = suppressWarnings(cor(bo, ag)),
    mean_x = mean(ex)
  )
}

betas   <- c(0.5, 1.0, 1.25, 2.0, 3.0)
results <- lapply(betas, run_one)

Observed pattern (seed 42, single replicate)

β n cor(exp, bold) cor(exp, aggro) cor(bold, aggro) mean(x)
0.50 676 +0.017 +0.058 +0.188 0.103
1.00 639 +0.071 −0.142 −0.025 0.102
1.25 670 +0.044 −0.005 +0.307 0.094
2.00 676 −0.054 +0.075 +0.124 0.097
3.00 641 −0.132 −0.063 −0.081 0.077

Three of five β values produce the predicted positive bold-aggro syndrome at this single seed; two produce essentially-zero or negative values. The syndrome strength varies by 0.39 in correlation units across the β range (single seed only — peak −0.08 at β = 3.0 to peak +0.31 at β = 1.25).

The asset-protection signs (cor(exp, bold) and cor(exp, aggro)) also shift with β at this seed: weakly positive at β = 0.5, becoming clearly negative at β = 3.0. This looked like Wolf’s predicted direction (high-x agents should be less bold / less aggressive) emerging at the high end of the β range — at one seed.

The single-seed reading suggested a clean β = 1.25 peak. But that read does not survive multi-seed scrutiny — see the next section.

Multi-seed verification (2026-05-16)

A multi-seed regression baseline was generated by dev/audit/fidelity/paper_massol_crochet_2008.R (5 β values × 8 seeds = 40 runs, ~22 min wall-clock). Saved to dev/audit/fidelity/paper_massol_crochet_2008.rds. Headline finding: β = 1.25 is the TROUGH, not the peak.

cor(bold, aggro) by β (8 seeds per cell)

β mean ± SE t verdict
0.5 −0.004 ± 0.067 −0.06 null
1.0 +0.003 ± 0.065 +0.05 null
1.25 −0.077 ± 0.049 −1.59 marginal-NEGATIVE
2.0 −0.003 ± 0.054 −0.05 null
3.0 +0.053 ± 0.040 +1.33 null
  • The single-seed peak (+0.307 at β = 1.25) is now marginal-negative (−0.077) at 8 seeds.
  • Range across β collapses from single-seed 0.39 to multi-seed 0.13 — Massol & Crochet’s “β-sensitive” claim is partially borne out (the range exists), but the effect is 3× smaller than the single seed suggested.
  • No β value produces a robust positive syndrome. The closest to PASS is β = 3.0 at t = +1.33, still null.

Asset-protection correlations across β

The cor(exp, bold) and cor(exp, aggro) means are all between ±0.07 across all β values; none reach 2σ. Wolf’s predicted negative signs don’t emerge robustly at any β. The single-seed “high β → predicted negative direction” pattern was noise.

Reading the result — honest revision

The original single-seed reading was: “Massol & Crochet’s critique is partially borne out — syndrome peaks at Wolf’s β = 1.25 and varies with β.” At 8 seeds:

  1. The β-sensitivity claim survives. Range exists (0.13 in correlation units), so the syndrome IS β-dependent. Massol & Crochet’s mathematical critique is consistent with this.
  2. The “peak at Wolf’s β = 1.25” claim is contradicted. At multi-seed scale β = 1.25 is the most negative condition of the five tested. The interpretation “Wolf chose a parameter that maximised the syndrome” doesn’t hold.
  3. No β produces Wolf’s predicted positive syndrome robustly. The fragility critique generalises further than Massol & Crochet themselves argued: not “fragile at suboptimal β” but “absent at all tested β” in clade’s spatial implementation.

Takeaway: clade’s spatial Wolf 2007 reproduction is null on the syndrome metric across the entire β sweep. Massol & Crochet’s critique is correct in spirit (the mechanism is parameter-fragile) but the specific quantitative claim — that Wolf’s β = 1.25 sits at the favourable end of the parameter range — is contradicted at multi-seed scrutiny. Documented as a candidate finding for the next kernel-review pass.

The two-correlation tension (syndrome peaks at intermediate β; asset- protection signs strengthen at high β) suggests clade’s spatial implementation produces two distinct selection regimes rather than one coherent dimorphism. Massol & Crochet’s framing — that the mathematical conditions for stable dimorphism are narrow — is consistent with what we see, but the spatial implementation adds its own structure: the population locks at x ≈ 0.1 at every β tested, so the true x dimorphism Wolf predicts (x ≈ 0 vs x ≈ 1 modes) is absent regardless.

Caveats

  • Single seed (42). The numbers above will move with seed-to-seed variation. A proper test would run 5–10 seeds per β and report distributions. The pattern is suggestive, not definitive.
  • Phase 6 denominator on. The runs include the per-strategy (1+α·N_i) competition denominator that strengthens the bold-aggro syndrome at Wolf’s β = 1.25 (see vignette("paper-wolf2007"), “Honest discussion of magnitudes”). Setting personality_alpha = 0 reverts to Phase 3a behaviour and would shift these numbers.
  • No bimodality at any β. Wolf’s headline result is a stable dimorphism (x ≈ 0 mode + x ≈ 1 mode). clade’s mean(x) ≈ 0.1 at every β; the population is unimodal at low x throughout the sweep. This matches the Phase 6 finding in paper-wolf2007.Rmd — clade’s spatial competition + one-per-cell offspring placement constrain the equilibrium to a single attractor.

What this implies for the Wolf 2007 reproduction

The 0.7.0 implementation of Wolf 2007 did reproduce the defining bold-aggro syndrome at Wolf’s parameter values. This vignette shows that the syndrome’s strength is genuinely sensitive to β — which is exactly what Massol & Crochet predicted. Future users of the Wolf scenario should treat the syndrome strength as parameter-dependent, not as a fixed property of the mechanism.

References

  • Massol, F. & Crochet, P. (2008) Do animal personalities emerge? Nature 451 (Brief Communications Arising). doi:10.1038/nature06744.
  • Wolf, M., van Doorn, G.S., Leimar, O. & Weissing, F.J. (2007) Life-history trade-offs favour the evolution of animal personalities. Nature 447:581–584. doi:10.1038/nature05835.
  • See also vignette("paper-wolf2007") for the base reproduction and the Phase 6 (1+α·N_i) denominator refinement that strengthens the syndrome at Wolf’s β = 1.25.
